title: Opening a File
ques: Write a snippet using `fopen` to open a file named "data.txt" in **write mode**. Always check if the file opened successfully.
explanation: `fopen` returns a FILE pointer. If it fails (e.g., permissions issue), it returns `NULL`. Mode "w" creates a new file or overwrites an existing one.

title: Writing Text to File
ques: Use `fprintf` to write the string **"Hello, File Handling!"** and an integer **2023** to the file opened in the previous exercise.
explanation: `fprintf` works like `printf` but takes a file pointer as the first argument to direct output to a file.

title: Reading Text from File
ques: Write a program to open "data.txt" in **read mode** and read the content using `fgets` line by line until end of file, printing it to the console.
explanation: `fgets` reads a line from the file into the buffer. It returns `NULL` when it hits the End Of File (EOF).

title: Appending to a File
ques: How do you open a file to **append** data (add to the end) without erasing existing content? Show the `fopen` mode.
explanation: Mode "a" opens the file for appending. Writing via `fprintf` or `fputs` will add data at the end of the file.

title: Writing Character by Character
ques: Use `fputc` to write the characters 'A', 'B', and 'C' to a file named "chars.txt".
explanation: `fputc(char, file_pointer)` writes a single character to the file stream.

title: Reading Formatted Data
ques: Assume a file "score.txt" contains "Alice 95". Use `fscanf` to read the name into a string and the score into an integer.
explanation: `fscanf` reads formatted input from a file, similar to how `scanf` reads from the console.

title: Binary Write (fwrite)
ques: Use `fwrite` to write an **array of 5 integers** `{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}` to a binary file "numbers.bin".
explanation: `fwrite(ptr, size, count, stream)` writes `count` elements, each of `size` bytes, from `ptr` to the file.

title: Binary Read (fread)
ques: Read the array written in the previous exercise from "numbers.bin" into a new array `readArr` using `fread` and print the first element.
explanation: `fread` reads raw binary data directly into memory. It is efficient for saving complex data structures.

title: File Positioning (fseek)
ques: Explain how to move the file cursor to the **beginning** of a file using `rewind` or `fseek`. Write a one-line example using `rewind`.
explanation: `rewind(fp)` sets the file position indicator to the beginning of the file. Equivalent to `fseek(fp, 0, SEEK_SET)`.
